name: Gate of the Sun
description: The Gate of the Sun (Puerta del Sol) is a monolithic stone archway located at Tiwanaku, an ancient archaeological site in Bolivia. It is one of the most iconic structures at Tiwanaku and has become a focal point for various alternative theories. Proponents suggest the Gate of the Sun is far more than a simple gateway, claiming it holds encoded astronomical and astrological knowledge, possibly even representing a portal or a key to understanding advanced ancient technologies or extraterrestrial contact. They point to the intricate carvings, particularly the central figure often identified as Viracocha, and the surrounding calendar-like symbols, as evidence of sophisticated scientific understanding beyond what mainstream archaeology attributes to the Tiwanaku culture. Some theories connect it to Atlantean or Lemurian civilizations, suggesting it predates Tiwanaku and was built by a more advanced civilization. Mainstream archaeology views the Gate of the Sun as a ceremonial gateway or calendar, built by the Tiwanaku culture between 500 and 900 AD. Scholars interpret the carvings as representations of deities, calendrical systems, and aspects of Tiwanaku cosmology. The central figure is generally identified as a deity, possibly Tunupa, and the surrounding figures are believed to represent calendar days or months. They attribute the perceived 'advanced' knowledge to the Tiwanaku's well-developed understanding of agriculture, astronomy, and engineering within their cultural context. The weathering and damage to the gate make precise interpretation challenging, contributing to the range of interpretations. Claims of advanced technology or extraterrestrial influence are generally dismissed by mainstream scholars as lacking empirical evidence and based on misinterpretations or selective readings of the available archaeological data.
subtitle: Ancient gateway of mystery and speculation

claims.claim: The Gate of the Sun encodes advanced astronomical knowledge.
claims.evidence: Intricate carvings resembling calendar systems and astronomical symbols.
claims.criticism: Mainstream archaeology interprets the carvings as representations of the Tiwanaku calendar and deities, not necessarily advanced astronomical knowledge beyond their cultural context.

claims.claim: The Gate of the Sun is a portal or gateway to another dimension.
claims.evidence: The enigmatic nature of the structure and the symbolic meaning of the archway.
claims.criticism: This claim lacks empirical evidence and is based on speculative interpretations of the gate's symbolism.

claims.claim: The Gate of the Sun was built by a civilization predating Tiwanaku, possibly Atlantean or Lemurian.
claims.evidence: The perceived sophistication of the stonework and the advanced knowledge encoded within the carvings.
claims.criticism: There is no archaeological evidence to support the existence of Atlantis or Lemuria, nor any evidence that the Gate predates the Tiwanaku culture.

keyFigures.name: Arthur Posnansky
keyFigures.role: Early researcher who proposed an older age for Tiwanaku

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Alternative historian who promotes theories of advanced ancient civilizations
